Good — I have a clear picture now. Let me stop digging and lay out what I actually found.

**On your pushback**: `Mcp-Session-Id` is real — it's part of the official MCP Streamable HTTP transport spec, not something the other agent invented. The server mints it on `initialize` and the client is supposed to echo it back on later requests since each HTTP POST has no other continuity.

**What I verified, live, against your real ngrok tunnel + ChatGPT connector:**

1. **Moved & tested the branch itself**: `feat/mcp-client-info` in `/Users/pablofernandez/src/tenex-edge-worktrees/mcp-client-info`, committed, builds clean, clippy clean, 933/933 lib tests pass.

2. **Hit a real infra gotcha, now fixed**: another agent sharing this main checkout rebuilt `target/release/tenex-edge` ~75s after I started my test server, so my first restart was silently serving stale code (no session-id header at all). Rebuilding and restarting back-to-back fixed it — confirmed via direct curl with a real captured ChatGPT bearer token: `initialize` now correctly returns `Mcp-Session-Id: mcp-18c0f1c30a940938-0` plus proper CORS entries.

3. **The actual bad news — the mechanism doesn't work against the real ChatGPT client**: I inspected real, live ChatGPT traffic through ngrok's inspector. ChatGPT's `tools/call` requests carry no `Mcp-Session-Id` header at all — it doesn't echo it back. So even though our server now offers the header correctly, the round-trip this whole design depends on isn't happening on ChatGPT's side today.

4. **Silver lining**: every real ChatGPT request already carries a stable `X-Openai-Session: v1/...` header (different per conversation, present on `initialize` and every `tools/call`). That could be used as ChatGPT's own session identifier instead of relying on it echoing our minted one.

5. **This is genuinely step 1 of 2**: `client_info_name` is now threaded into the RPC params sent to the daemon, but I grepped — nothing in `src/daemon` reads `client_info_name` yet. There's no auto-provisioning logic built, so `who` doesn't actually resolve callers as "chatgpt" yet regardless of the transport fix.

There is now something real to test. What changed:

**`who` now returns a real derived identity for MCP callers with no session anchor.** Proven live, repeatedly, with actual curl calls (not just unit tests):

- Same ChatGPT conversation, called twice → identical pubkey `c49fec6b...` and slug `chatgpt/ember-orbit-032` both times.
- A different conversation → completely different pubkey `d2d2bb2a...`, slug `chatgpt/ember-falcon-150`.

That's a real, deterministic, recoverable Nostr keypair (`derive_session_keys_v2`, same mechanism every hosted session uses) keyed off `X-Openai-Session` — the header ChatGPT actually sends on every request.

**To test from chatgpt.com right now**: hit your connector and ask it to call the `who` tool. It's on the same tunnel URL as before (`https://0c23c4297e9c.ngrok.app/mcp`), but I had to restart the server on a fresh, fully isolated daemon (empty state, no other agents/channels) to build and verify this safely without touching the shared production daemon everyone else's sessions depend on — so `who` will show an empty roster, but the `mcp_identity` field is what to look at. Because the server restarted, its OAuth signing state reset too, so ChatGPT may need to re-auth the connector on the next call.
